national_day.intro: Singapore's National Day on 9 August celebrates the nation's independence in 1965.
national_day.history: On 9 August 1965 Singapore separated from Malaysia and became an independent, sovereign republic. From a small port city it grew rapidly into one of the world's wealthiest nations, a transformation celebrated each year.
national_day.traditions: The highlight is the National Day Parade, with military and civilian processions, an air force flypast and a spectacular fireworks display over Marina Bay. Singaporeans recite the National Pledge and sing along to a specially commissioned theme song.
funFacts: It marks Singapore's 1965 separation from Malaysia.
The National Day Parade features a dramatic flypast and fireworks.
Citizens recite the National Pledge together at 8:22 pm.

chinese_new_year.intro: Chinese New Year is one of Singapore's biggest festivals, a two-day public holiday welcoming the lunar new year.
chinese_new_year.history: Brought by Chinese immigrants who form the majority of Singapore's population, Chinese New Year marks the start of the lunar calendar. Over generations it has become a defining national celebration in multicultural Singapore.
chinese_new_year.traditions: Families gather for reunion dinners and toss "yusheng", a prosperity salad, for good luck. Chinatown is decorated with lights, children receive red packets (hongbao), and lion dances are performed for prosperity.
funFacts: Tossing "yusheng" salad higher is said to bring greater fortune.
Red packets called "hongbao" contain money for luck.
Chinatown's annual light-up draws huge crowds.
